name: MongoDB Atlas — sharded cluster, caching workload (uniform), YCSB (benchANT 2023)
notes: : the first MEASURED latency-at-load corpus entry — the meanLatencyMsAtLoad metric the corpus previously had no scored point for (capabilities.ts's 'tail, below-knee' cell was 'scorable but awaits one'). Source: benchANT's open-source MongoDB-vs-ScyllaDB benchmark (github.com/benchANT/mongodb-vs-scylladb), the 'caching' workload, uniform key distribution, configuration 'mongodb-large-48k' — MongoDB Atlas 6.0, SHARDED cluster of 6x M200_NVME (AWS us-west-2), driven by 8 YCSB CoreWorkload loader nodes each issuing '-target 6000' (48,000 op/s nominal combined), 50/50 read/update, uniform distribution, ~1 KB records (fieldCount 10 x fieldLength 100), 1800 s measurement window, writeConcern majority+journal, readPreference primary. RE-VERIFIED directly from the raw per-run YCSB output files (0_run.txt..7_run.txt): summing each run's [OVERALL] Throughput(ops/sec) gives a combined 44,898.40 op/s (93.54% of the 48,000 nominal target — the loaders could not sustain their target rate, the classic signature of a server-side ceiling, corroborated qualitatively by the ScyllaDB engineering blog's own narrative for this exact pairing ('MongoDB serves only... under a comparable cluster utilization of 80-90%')); averaging each run's [READ] AverageLatency(us) gives 3253.77 us (3.2538 ms), and each run's [UPDATE] AverageLatency(us) gives 24,366.43 us (24.3664 ms) — both match the figure this task was briefed with. CORRECTION to the briefing: the source evaluationScenario.json for this configuration records recordCount 10,000,000,000 (not '500M') — the 500M figure describes the PAIRED ScyllaDB-small config's dataset, not this one; noted here as an honest correction, though dataset size plays no role in the fitted arithmetic below. HONEST METRIC CHOICE: the workload is exactly 50/50 read/update (readProportion 0.5, updateProportion 0.5), so the scored ground truth is the BLENDED per-operation mean — (READ_mean + UPDATE_mean)/2 = (3.2538 + 24.3664)/2 = 13.8101 ms — the honest counterpart of SDA's single per-node responseLatency (one node, one service-time distribution; it cannot separately track a 3.25 ms read and a 24.4 ms update on the same connection pool). The per-op means are recorded here as context: the read/update asymmetry (7.5x) is a KNOWN model simplification and part of the honest residual — this scalar validates the BLENDED mean, not the per-operation-type split. UNDERDETERMINATION GUARD: one (rate, mean) point cannot free both the pool width and the held time (capacity = pool/held is degenerate along the ratio) — following the corpus's existing Cassandra-ceiling precedent, the pool is PINNED from a sourced fact scaled by the cluster's documented topology (see the connectionPool tunable note) and connectionHeldMs is FITTED. The actual `pnpm calibrate` fit lands at connectionHeldMs = 13.1014 ms, rho = 0.9804 (< 1, verified) — post-fit residual -0.9% (grid-search granularity, see CALIBRATION-REPORT.md) — a physically sensible held time (low-teens of milliseconds) for a blended cache-tier read/update mix on a cluster running near-saturation (matching the independent blog corroboration above), not a degenerate corner. COUPLING DECISION: this entry's tunables are NODE-scoped (selector.node = 'mongo'), NOT type-scoped (type: db.mongodb) — so they share NOTHING with DeathStarBench's db.mongodb:connectionHeldMs (a type-scoped tunable calibrating a near-idle, catalog-default-scale single Mongo instance inside a low-load microservices benchmark). These are two DIFFERENT deployments at two different eras and scales (DSB 2019 ASPLOS lab rig vs this 2023 production-scale Atlas sharded cluster near saturation) — sharing one connectionHeldMs value across them would poison both fits (DSB's ~8.5%-share anchor needs a small, near-catalog-default hold time; this entry's blended mean needs an order-of-magnitude larger one), so each keeps its own model-level tunable, exactly the corpus's existing per-model-override mechanism (see the Cassandra 2017 write-ceiling entry, which pins its own pool+held independently of any other db.cassandra entry). SCOPE: single scored point (no leave-one-out partner yet for this exact metric/deployment — the honest disjoint fallback applies until a second benchANT-family latency-at-load point joins the corpus); config-sensitive (one specific Atlas tier, one specific workload mix) the same way the Redis/Cassandra ceiling entries are.

sources.note: The 8 raw YCSB run outputs (0_run.txt .. 7_run.txt) for the 'mongodb-large-48k' configuration — each carries [OVERALL] Throughput(ops/sec), [READ] AverageLatency(us), [UPDATE] AverageLatency(us). Re-verified directly: summed throughput 44,898.40 op/s; simple-average READ 3,253.77 us; simple-average UPDATE 24,366.43 us (op counts ~5.05M per run per type, near-equal, so the simple average is a faithful stand-in for the op-weighted mean).

The benchmark configuration: MongoDB Atlas 6.0, dbaasProvider ATLAS, SHARDED cluster, numShards 6, instanceSizeName M200_NVME, region US_WEST_2, writeConcern {w: majority, j: true}, readPreference primary; benchmarkNodes.numberOfNodes 8; YCSB CoreWorkload targetOps 48000, readProportion 0.5, updateProportion 0.5, requestdistribution UNIFORM, fieldCount 10, fieldLength 100, maxExecutionTime 1800.

ScyllaDB engineering blog's own deep-dive on this exact caching benchmark pairing: reports the large-cluster comparison as 'ScyllaDB achieves 894 kOps/s against 45 kOps/s of MongoDB' (45k rounds to this entry's measured 44,898 op/s) and describes MongoDB's cluster utilization at this point as '80-90%' — independent qualitative corroboration that the achieved rate sits close to the server-side ceiling, consistent with this entry's fitted rho ~= 0.98.
benchANT's own summary blog for the same study (narrative cross-check only; no additional numbers beyond the raw results used here).

tunables.note: PINNED, not fitted: MongoDB's documented default maxPoolSize (100 connections per client-to-shard route — MONGO_POOL_SOURCE, the same source db.mongodb's catalog default already cites) x 6 shards (this cluster's own documented numShards, evaluationScenario.json). Capacity = pool/held is degenerate along the ratio, so the sourced structural fact is pinned and connectionHeldMs is fitted, the same guard the Cassandra write-ceiling entry follows. NODE-scoped (selector.node, not selector.type) so this pin never couples with any other db.mongodb entry's pool.

FITTED: the per-operation connection-hold time that, at the pinned 600-slot pool and the achieved 44,898.4 op/s load, reproduces the measured 13.8101 ms blended read/update mean via the M/M/c sojourn (Erlang-C). Range capped at 13.3 ms — just below the pool's own saturation cliff (600 slots / 44,898.4 op/s = 13.36 ms, where rho would reach 1) so the search never wanders into an infeasible (rho>=1) region. NODE-scoped (selector.node = 'mongo'), deliberately NOT selector.type: 'db.mongodb' — sharing that id with DeathStarBench's type-scoped connectionHeldMs tunable would force ONE hold time to explain both a near-idle DSB instance and this near-saturated Atlas cluster, poisoning both fits (see notes).

groundTruth.note: The BLENDED mean latency (READ 3.2538 ms + UPDATE 24.3664 ms, averaged — the workload is exactly 50/50) at the achieved combined load of 44,898.4 op/s (93.5% of the 48,000 nominal target), re-verified from the 8 raw YCSB run files. SDA's out-of-box estimate (catalog default connectionHeldMs 5 ms on a 50-slot pool) predicts a near-idle sojourn far below the measured value; the fit corrects the pool (pinned, sourced) and the hold time (fitted) to reproduce it, landing at rho ~= 0.98 — a real, verified-sub-1 utilization, not a saturated design. The per-op split (3.25 ms read vs 24.4 ms update, a 7.5x spread) is NOT separately scored: SDA models one node with one service-time distribution, so that asymmetry is the honest residual this scalar cannot remove.
